RiskFactors.full_evidence: Signature verification bypass — critical bridge vulnerability
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This factor shows that signature verification bypass — critical bridge vulnerability. Investors should treat the protocol as exploitable until the issue is publicly remediated.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Harden the contracts so that signature verification bypass — critical bridge vulnerability is no longer possible. Deploy targeted tests, guards, and emergency procedures before redeploying.

cir_explanation.title: Bridge Signature Verification Bypass
cir_explanation.summary: Ensure bridge handlers verify unique nonces and enforce signature thresholds before calling mint or withdraw functions.

guidance.investor: The bridge's signature verification — the process that confirms withdrawals are legitimate — can be bypassed. Bridges require multiple signers to approve transactions (like a safe needing multiple keys). When replay protection or threshold checks are missing, an attacker can reuse old approvals or forge new ones to drain funds. The Ronin Bridge lost $625M through a similar signature bypass. Do not hold significant value on bridges where this weakness exists.
guidance.developer: Hash payloads with domain-separated replay IDs, enforce minimum signature thresholds, and reject already-processed message hashes before executing withdrawals.

RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This bridge has multiple vulnerability classes that compound each other's risk. Cross-chain bridges custody enormous value and are prime targets for large-scale attacks — the Ronin Bridge ($625M), Poly Network ($611M), and Wormhole ($320M) hacks all exploited overlapping weaknesses. When a protocol exhibits signature verification weaknesses and cross-chain message validation gaps, an attacker can chain these in a single transaction to bypass defences that would individually hold. The 1.3x multiplier reflects this compounding: each additional attack surface doesn't just add risk, it multiplies it.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Address both vulnerability vectors simultaneously — fixing one in isolation leaves the other exploitable, and attackers will chain them. Vectors identified: signature verification weaknesses and cross-chain message validation gaps. Implement defence-in-depth across signature validation, message verification, validator key management, and execution controls. If any of these vectors are unclear, commission an independent security audit before deploying.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Bridge architecture contains known vulnerability patterns
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This bridge's architecture shares characteristics with bridges that have been exploited in the past. Cross-chain bridges are among the highest-risk components in DeFi, responsible for billions in losses (Ronin $625M, Wormhole $320M, Poly Network $611M). The complexity of coordinating security across multiple chains creates attack surfaces that don't exist in single-chain protocols. Limit exposure and monitor bridge security announcements closely.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Conduct a thorough security review comparing your architecture against known bridge exploits. Key areas to audit: validator key management, message verification logic, replay protection, and upgrade mechanisms. Consider implementing defense-in-depth: rate limiting, pause mechanisms, and monitoring systems that can detect anomalies before full exploitation.

cir_explanation.title: Bridge architecture vulnerabilities
cir_explanation.summary: The bridge's overall design — its validator management, upgrade mechanisms, and security layering — contains structural patterns that have been exploited in previous bridge hacks.

guidance.investor: This bridge's architecture shares characteristics with bridges that have been exploited in the past. Cross-chain bridges are among the highest-risk components in DeFi, responsible for billions in losses (Ronin $625M, Wormhole $326M, Nomad $190M). The complexity of coordinating security across multiple chains creates attack surfaces that do not exist in single-chain protocols. Limit exposure and monitor bridge security announcements closely.
guidance.developer: Implement layered validation: gateway check, source whitelist, payload hash verification, nonce tracking, and rate limiting. Log every processed message for monitoring.

RiskFactors.full_evidence: Bridge message processing vulnerable to manipulation attacks
RiskFactors.explanation_investors: This bridge may accept cross-chain messages without adequately verifying their authenticity or origin. Attackers could potentially forge messages to unlock or mint assets they don't own. This class of vulnerability was exploited in the Wormhole ($320M) and Poly Network ($611M) hacks. Treat this as a critical risk indicator.
RiskFactors.explanation_developers: Implement comprehensive message validation: verify source chain ID, sender address, nonce for replay protection, and cryptographic signatures from a sufficient validator set. Use structured data formats (like EIP-712) that prevent type confusion attacks. Add sanity checks on amounts and recipients. Consider implementing fraud proofs or challenge periods for large transfers.

cir_explanation.title: Bridge message manipulation risk
cir_explanation.summary: Cross-chain message handlers process incoming payloads without fully verifying the source chain, sender address, or message integrity — enabling forged messages to trigger withdrawals or mints.

guidance.investor: This bridge may accept cross-chain messages without adequately verifying their authenticity or origin. Attackers could forge messages to unlock or mint assets they do not own. This class of vulnerability was central to the Wormhole ($326M) and Poly Network ($611M) exploits. Treat this as a critical risk — do not bridge significant value until message validation has been independently audited.
guidance.developer: Use structured data formats (like EIP-712) that prevent type confusion attacks. Validate gateway, source chain, and source address against a whitelist before processing any cross-chain payload.

exploit_meta.name: Ronin Bridge
exploit_meta.description: Compromise of 5 out of 9 validator private keys

sections.title: Not a Bug — a Key Heist
sections.text: March 23, 2022: North Korea’s Lazarus Group executes the largest bridge exploit in crypto history — not by finding a smart contract bug, but by stealing validator keys. Ronin’s Ethereum bridge ran Proof-of-Authority consensus requiring 5 of 9 validator signatures to authorise withdrawals. Sky Mavis operated four validators; Axie DAO held one; four others were distributed to external parties. The attackers compromised all four Sky Mavis keys through social engineering — spear-phishing and malware targeting internal systems — then reached the fifth through a lingering operational shortcut.

sections.title: The Forgotten Allowlist
sections.text: In late 2021, Axie DAO had temporarily allowed Sky Mavis to sign transactions on its behalf to handle a period of high network load. The allowlist was never fully revoked. This meant that whoever controlled Sky Mavis’s infrastructure effectively controlled five of nine validators — the exact threshold needed to authorise bridge withdrawals. The attackers used these five compromised keys to sign two withdrawal transactions: 173,600 ETH and 25.5 million USDC, totalling approximately $625M. From the bridge contract’s perspective, these were perfectly valid — five legitimate signatures, threshold met.

sections.title: Six Days in the Dark
sections.text: The bridge had no real-time anomaly detection, no withdrawal rate limiting, and no alerts for large outflows. The exploit went undetected for six days. It was only discovered on March 29 when a user tried to withdraw 5,000 ETH and the transaction failed — the bridge was insolvent. Six days of silence on a $625M theft. The stolen funds were laundered through Tornado Cash, cross-chain bridges, and eventually centralised exchanges — a pattern consistent with prior Lazarus Group operations. Law enforcement and Chainalysis later recovered approximately $30M.

sections.title: The Single-Org Failure Domain
sections.text: The FBI and U.S. Treasury formally attributed the attack to North Korea’s Lazarus Group. Sky Mavis raised $150M (led by Binance) to reimburse users, expanded the validator set, raised the signature threshold, and removed the legacy allowlist. But the core lesson is architectural: a 5-of-9 multisig where one organisation controls four keys and has a backdoor path to a fifth is a single-organisation failure domain. Notably, our engine flagged vulnerabilities in this contract that the attackers never even needed to exploit — missing replay protection in the signature verification path, and structural weaknesses in the bridge’s message validation. Lazarus took a simpler door, but the code had others.

breakdown.vulnerability: Stolen validator keys via social engineering — 5-of-9 threshold breached
breakdown.steps: 1) Lazarus Group compromises four Sky Mavis validator keys via spear-phishing and internal system infiltration. 2) A residual allowlist from late 2021 lets Sky Mavis sign on behalf of Axie DAO — attackers now control 5 of 9 keys. 3) Sign two fraudulent withdrawals: 173,600 ETH and 25.5M USDC ($625M total). 4) Bridge contract validates 5 signatures, threshold met — funds released. 5) Exploit goes undetected for 6 days until a user’s 5,000 ETH withdrawal fails. 6) Funds laundered via Tornado Cash and cross-chain bridges; ~$30M later recovered by law enforcement.
